segments.text: Remember the good old days when all you
could do with your chat interfaces is
interact with text, right? It's like six
months ago, a year ago when MCP came out
and you got all excited about plugging
all these MCP servers, but all you could
do is really text, right? You would ask
for some data visualization and you'd
get a blob of JSON that you can have to
copy paste, put in Excel, render a chart
and just do a lot of this manual work.
And those days, my friends, they're over
starting today because starting today,
there is a new official extension to
model context protocol called MCP apps.
MCP apps allows you to bring
interactivity, yes, that's right, real
interactivity inside the chat interface.
So, you're no longer constrained to just
text. You can actually render things as
you would in a web app. uh and that is
generally available through uh our MCP
apps extension. And when I say it's an
extension, this means it's not part of
the core protocol, but it is something
that you can still use and still see
integrated with a lot of the MCP
clients. Now, this work has been cooking
up for a while and of course it's built
on a massive industry effort. So there's
contributions from OpenAI here. There's
contributions from MCPUI. And if you've
been in the MCP contributors discord,
you might have even, you know, lurked on
these conversations. So if the names uh
Solomon or Lead Ysef ring a bell to you,
well, congratulations. These are the
folks that have been building the
foundations of this effort. So, I am
super excited about this because now all
of the ideas that I had about MCP
servers actually rendering UI inside of
the chat interfaces and having this
interactivity with the users where I
don't have to rely on just text and
actually see things, right? I'm a visual
person. I want to see things render. I
want to see the charts. I want to see
some kind of button that I can click and
then see the reaction. That's great.
